text: Severe Endometriosis Intrinsic Ureteral Disease We describe a thirty years old woman with primary infertility, right back lower pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia and dyscaesia. The urography shows the uretero-edonephrosis on the right side. The MRI revealed the stenosis of the ureter at the level of right ovarian fossa. The renal scintigraphy showed a forty percent kidney function on the right side.

During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon uses bipolar forceps to detach the bowel from the abdominal wall, carefully following the white line to avoid entering the retroperitoneal space. The bowel is then fixed to the anterior abdominal wall using a T-lift. The left ovary, initially adhered to the bowel, is gently separated from it without electrosurgery, using traction to achieve separation.

The surgeon fixes the left ovary onto the bottom wall using a D-lift. The left cloretal fossa is opened with a bipolar device and irrigation, employing traction and contra-traction movements. Bubbles from neomodissection guide the process. The right parietal fossa is opened to isolate the rectum bilaterally, with careful dissection to avoid vessel or nerve damage. The rectovaginal nodule is approached cautiously to prevent bowel injury.

The surgeon carefully opens the rectovaginal space using scissors, leaving the nodule attached to the posterior vagina. Ureterolysis is performed to clearly identify the ureter, revealing endometriosis causing stenosis and compression. The surrounding endometriotic tissue is visible, infiltrating the ureter wall and causing retraction, while efforts are made to preserve the ureter's vascularization.

The surgeon uses a monocular hook and a grasper with bipolar capability to remove the retrovaginal endometriotic nodule. A glove with gauzes is inserted into the vagina to prevent gas leakage. The vaginal incision is closed with a continuous polyfilament absorbable suture. The integrity of the bowel is tested by injecting air into the rectum and observing for any perforation, with saline in the pelvic cavity, resulting in a negative test.

Following urologic evaluation, the surgeon performs a uretero-ureterostomy. A DJ stent is introduced using a cystoscopic approach. The segment of the ureter affected by endometriosis is excised, and the two ends of the ureter are reattached using interrupted monofilament absorbable sutures. The excised ureteric segment is sent for histological analysis.
